channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Judge Orders Construction Stopped on Trump’s White House Ballroom
item.description: A federal judge required the president to seek lawmakers’ input and pursue traditional approvals before proceeding with the $400 million replacement for the East Wing.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: A federal judge ordered on Tuesday that construction be halted of the White House ballroom.

item.title: Trump Signs Order Seeking Federal Control of Mail Voting as He Promotes False Claims
item.description: Election experts and Democratic officials called the order legally invalid, and Arizona and Oregon pledged to immediately challenge it in court.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti and Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times

item.title: Justice Dept. Struggles to Respond to Trump’s Suit Against IRS
item.description: Officials at the department and the White House are in the middle of a messy and complicated debate over how to respond to President Trump’s lawsuit demanding $10 billion from the I.R.S.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren and Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump filed a lawsuit demanding at least $10 billion from the I.R.S.

item.title: Trump Says U.S. Will Be Out of Iran Within Two to Three Weeks
item.description: The White House said the president would address the nation about Iran on Wednesday evening.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager and Edward Wong
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump told reporters on Tuesday that he had attained his goal of dealing with Iran’s nuclear program and that gasoline prices in the United States would be coming down.

item.title: Trump Says He Halted Nuclear Threat From Iran, Despite Evidence to the Contrary
item.description: For the second time in recent days, President Trump declared that one of the key objectives of the war had been accomplished.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has narrowed his Iran war objectives in recent days.

item.title: Trump Seeks to Redefine ‘Regime Change’ in Iran War
item.description: President Trump and his aides have made contradictory statements on whether the United States and Israel have transformed the Iranian government through violence.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said during his news conference on Tuesday that “this new regime, because regime change has occurred, should be wiser than the last.”

item.title: The Sailors Stranded in the Persian Gulf
item.description: Thousands of civilian sailors have been stranded for more than a month in waters surrounded by a conflict zone because of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
item.dc:creator: Aie Balagtas See
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Altaf Qadri/Associated Press
item.media:description: Almost 20,000 seafarers are stranded in the Persian Gulf, according to the International Maritime Organization, as Iran has effectively blocked the exit through the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Backdoor Funding of Homeland Security Agency Could Weaken Congress Anew
item.description: An emerging Republican plan to skirt a Democratic filibuster and fund an entire department without congressional appropriations would be the latest example of surrendering power to the White House.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Border Patrol agents in Minneapolis in early January. Using the complex budget process known as reconciliation to fund the entire Homeland Security Department, as Republicans are suggesting, would be a significant departure from traditional congressional practice.

item.title: Who Is Getting Paid During the Department of Homeland Security Shutdown?
item.description: At least 120,000 law enforcement officers who work for the agency have continued to collect paychecks throughout the funding lapse. But tens of thousands of workers have gone without pay.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo
item.media:credit: Gene J. Puskar/Associated Press
item.media:description: A T.S.A. agent checks passengers at the security checkpoint at Pittsburgh International Airport on Monday.

item.title: South Dakotans React to Daily Mail Article on Bryon Noem, Kristi Noem’s Husband
item.description: In the tiny town of Castlewood, S.D., where everyone knows the Noems, the prevailing sense was that people can’t help but feel bad for Bryon Noem after a tabloid photo leak.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.media:credit: Ben Brewer for The New York Times
item.media:description: Castlewood is not far from the Noem family farm in South Dakota.

item.title: Gavin Newsom Suggests His Conservative Critics Are Gay in Online Insults Criticized as Homophobic
item.description: Gov. Gavin Newsom’s aides said that their online insults were meant to ridicule figures on the right. But some critics say they are homophobic.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Felix Hörhager/picture-alliance, via AP Images
item.media:description: Gov. Gavin Newsom of California, a potential 2028 presidential candidate, is attracting criticism for social media posts taunting his opponents by suggesting they are gay.

item.title: Judge Dismisses Lawsuit That Challenged Ban on Endorsements by Churches
item.description: Conservatives had expected a victory in the case after the I.R.S. agreed to a settlement that allowed churches to voice support for candidates.
item.dc:creator: David A. Fahrenthold and Elizabeth Dias
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: A federal judge in Texas on Tuesday dismissed a lawsuit seeking to overturn the 70-year-old ban on political activity by churches.

item.title: Where Million-Dollar Coastal Homes Glitter Near Metal Shacks
item.description: A shortage of affordable housing in the coastal city in South Africa has forced many people to live far outside the city center, while tourists occupy prime real estate.
item.dc:creator: John Eligon and Joao Silva
item.media:credit: Joao Silva/The New York Times

item.title: Supreme Court Rejects Colorado Law Banning ‘Conversion Therapy’ for L.G.B.T.Q. Minors
item.description: The state and more than 20 others restrict therapists from trying to change the gender identity or sexual orientation of clients under 18.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Rachel Woolf for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kaley Chiles, a licensed professional counselor, at her office in Colorado Springs in September. Mrs. Chiles filed suit in 2022 over Colorado’s ban on conversion therapy for minors.

item.title: TMZ Goes After Members of Congress Living It Up Amid DHS Shutdown
item.description: After lawmakers left Washington for a two-week spring break with the Department of Homeland Security shut down, the Hollywood tabloid began publishing photographs of them living it up around the country.
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times

item.title: In UK Prisons, Drones Fly In Contraband ‘As if by Uber Eats’
item.description: Decades-old prison buildings were designed to be secure from the ground but not the air. Experts say that makes a lucrative smuggling trade hard to tackle.
item.dc:creator: Lizzie Dearden

item.title: At the National Archives, the Declaration Gets More Company
item.description: The Emancipation Proclamation and the 19th Amendment have been added to the Archives’s rotunda, the first permanent changes there in nearly 75 years.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Schuessler
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Visitors looked at the original copy of the 19th Amendment this week. The document was recently added to the permanent display in the rotunda at the National Archives in Washington.

item.title: Yes, This Is Your War, Too
item.description: Even opponents of the conflict should want to see Iran defanged and defeated.
item.dc:creator: Bret Stephens
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Photo illustration by The New York Times

item.title: Padma Lakshmi: The Decision That Would Create a Permanent American Underclass
item.description: President Trump wants to take away a fundamental American promise.
item.dc:creator: Padma Lakshmi
item.media:credit: Chuck Ramirez

item.title: Stephen Colbert Blames ‘The Bachelorette’ Cancellation for $4 Gas
item.description: The “Late Show” host joked that “Trump attacked Iran” after ABC scrapped the reality show’s latest season.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Tugboat Captain Is Charged in Miami Beach Crash That Killed 3 Children
item.description: Six people, including a camp counselor, were thrown into the water on July 28 when their sailboat was struck by a barge that was being pushed by a tugboat, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Johnny Diaz and Mark Walker
item.media:credit: Joe Raedle/Getty Images
item.media:description: An aerial view of Biscayne Bay surrounding Hibiscus Island in Miami Beach, where a barge struck a sailboat in July, killing three children.

item.title: Allbirds, Once Silicon Valley’s Favorite Shoe, Sells for $39 Million
item.description: Despite once being valued at $4 billion, the company that made sneakers from Merino wool struggled to capture a wide customer base and turn a profit.
item.dc:creator: Kim Bhasin
item.media:credit: Jeenah Moon for The New York Times
item.media:description: An Allbirds store in Manhattan in 2018. The brand has closed all of its U.S. stores.

item.title: Slava Tsukerman, Who Directed the Cult Classic ‘Liquid Sky,’ Dies at 86
item.description: A Russian-born director, he created a film about New Wave models and killer aliens in 1980s New York, helping to reshape independent filmmaking in America.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
